content.text: An agent team is a group of agents that collaborate on a common task. In AutoGen, a team is typically implemented as a
— a shared conversation space with a manager that routes tasks to the right specialist. Teams can also be nested hierarchically: an outer team's manager can delegate to an inner team (another
) that works privately and returns a single result. This lets you build scalable pipelines where each team has clear expertise boundaries.

attrs.alt: AutoGen hierarchical agent teams: outer manager delegates to a Research Team group chat and an Engineering Team group chat, each with their own internal agents and sub-manager

content.text: The most common team pattern: a user proxy starts a group chat with a manager and several specialist agents. Each specialist has a focused system message so the manager can reliably route tasks.

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent, GroupChat, GroupChatManager

llm_config = {"config_list": [{"model": "gpt-4o-mini", "api_key": "sk-..."}]}

# Specialists with tight, single-purpose system messages
researcher = AssistantAgent("researcher", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a research specialist. Gather and summarise factual information "
        "on any topic asked. Be concise and cite sources when possible."
    ))
coder = AssistantAgent("coder", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a Python expert. Write clean, runnable code. "
        "Include docstrings. Do not run the code yourself."
    ))
reviewer = AssistantAgent("reviewer", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a code reviewer. Check code for bugs, style issues, and "
        "missing edge cases. Suggest concrete improvements."
    ))
user_proxy = UserProxyAgent(
    "user_proxy",
    human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config={"work_dir": "team_ws", "use_docker": False},
    is_termination_msg=lambda m: "TERMINATE" in (m.get("content") or ""),
)

team = GroupChat(
    agents=[user_proxy, researcher, coder, reviewer],
    messages=[],
    max_round=15,
    speaker_selection_method="auto",
)
manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=team, llm_config=llm_config)
user_proxy.initiate_chat(
    manager,
    message=(
        "Research the merge sort algorithm, implement it in Python, "
        "and have it reviewed for correctness."
    ),
)
For complex tasks, nest one group chat inside another. The outer manager delegates to an inner team manager (a
). The inner team works privately; only the final result surfaces in the outer conversation.

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent, GroupChat, GroupChatManager

llm_config = {"config_list": [{"model": "gpt-4o-mini", "api_key": "sk-..."}]}

# ---- Inner Engineering Team ----
inner_coder = AssistantAgent("inner_coder", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You write Python code.")
inner_reviewer = AssistantAgent("inner_reviewer", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You review Python code for correctness.")
inner_executor = UserProxyAgent("inner_executor", human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config={"work_dir": "inner_ws", "use_docker": False},
    is_termination_msg=lambda m: "TERMINATE" in (m.get("content") or ""))

inner_team = GroupChat(
    agents=[inner_executor, inner_coder, inner_reviewer],
    messages=[],
    max_round=10,
)
inner_manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=inner_team, llm_config=llm_config)

# ---- Outer Team ----
# The engineering_liaison triggers the inner group chat via a nested chat
engineering_liaison = AssistantAgent("engineering_liaison", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You coordinate engineering tasks with the inner team.")

engineering_liaison.register_nested_chats(
    chat_queue=[{
        "sender": inner_executor,
        "recipient": inner_manager,
        "message": lambda s, r, ctx: ctx.get("last_message", {}).get("content", ""),
        "max_turns": 10,
        "summary_method": "reflection_with_llm",
    }],
    trigger=lambda sender: True,  # always delegate to inner team
)

researcher = AssistantAgent("researcher", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You research topics.")
outer_proxy = UserProxyAgent("outer_proxy", human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config=False,
    is_termination_msg=lambda m: "TERMINATE" in (m.get("content") or ""))

outer_team = GroupChat(
    agents=[outer_proxy, researcher, engineering_liaison],
    messages=[],
    max_round=6,
    speaker_selection_method="auto",
)
outer_manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=outer_team, llm_config=llm_config)
outer_proxy.initiate_chat(
    outer_manager,
    message="Research quicksort and have the engineering team implement it.",
)
Well-designed teams assign each agent a role that is mutually exclusive with the others. Common patterns:
Break task into steps, assign to others
Run code, call APIs, provide human input
Using Agent Descriptions for Better Selection
attribute on an agent is what the group chat manager sees when deciding who speaks next — separate from the
that the agent uses to generate replies. Write short, role-focused descriptions to guide the auto selector.

researcher = AssistantAgent(
    "researcher",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You are a research expert. Search the web and summarise concisely.",
    description="Use researcher when factual information needs to be gathered or verified.",
)
coder = AssistantAgent(
    "coder",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You write Python code to solve tasks. Include docstrings.",
    description="Use coder when Python code needs to be written, modified, or debugged.",
)
reviewer = AssistantAgent(
    "reviewer",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You review code for bugs, style, and correctness.",
    description="Use reviewer after code has been written to check it before execution.",
)
the manager's speaker-selection prompt lists each agent's
. The agent's reply loop uses its
. These can and should be different — descriptions are short routing hints; system messages are detailed instructions.
A team conversation ends when TERMINATE arrives or

is reached. For multi-step workflows, use a dedicated termination agent that only speaks at the end to signal completion — preventing early termination when an intermediate agent uses TERMINATE in a different context.

# A dedicated finaliser agent that only says TERMINATE when the task is fully done
finaliser = AssistantAgent(
    "finaliser",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You only speak when the task is fully complete. "
        "When all required work (research + code + review) is done, "
        "write a brief summary and end with TERMINATE. "
        "Do not speak before all steps are completed."
    ),
    description="Use finaliser only after researcher, coder, and reviewer have all finished.",
)

team = GroupChat(
    agents=[user_proxy, researcher, coder, reviewer, finaliser],
    messages=[],
    max_round=20,
    # Allowed transitions: finaliser can only follow reviewer
    allowed_or_disallowed_speaker_transitions={
        reviewer: [finaliser, user_proxy],
        finaliser: [],  # finaliser never continues — it ends the conversation
    },
    speaker_transitions_type="allowed",
)
After a conversation, analyse the message log to count how many times each agent spoke, how many rounds the conversation took, and whether the task was completed in the expected number of steps.

from collections import Counter

user_proxy.initiate_chat(manager, message="Research and implement a binary search tree.")

# Count contributions per agent
speakers = [m.get("name") for m in team.messages if m.get("name")]
counts = Counter(speakers)
for name, count in counts.most_common():
    print(f"{name:20s}: {count} messages")

total_rounds = len(team.messages)
print(f"Total rounds: {total_rounds} / {team.max_round}")

In AutoGen, tools (functions the LLM can request to be called) are registered per agent. For a team, you typically give each specialist the tools it needs: the researcher gets a web search tool, the coder gets nothing (it only writes), and the executor runs code. Register tools using

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent, GroupChat, GroupChatManager

llm_config = {"config_list": [{"model": "gpt-4o-mini", "api_key": "sk-..."}]}

def web_search(query: str) -> str:
    """Simulate a web search — replace with a real API call."""
    return f"Search results for '{query}': [result1, result2, result3]"

researcher = AssistantAgent("researcher", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You gather information using the web_search tool.",
    description="Use researcher when facts or background information are needed.")

user_proxy = UserProxyAgent("user_proxy", human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config=False,
    is_termination_msg=lambda m: "TERMINATE" in (m.get("content") or ""))

# Register the tool: LLM sees it on researcher, proxy executes it
researcher.register_for_llm(name="web_search", description="Search the web for information")(web_search)
user_proxy.register_for_execution(name="web_search")(web_search)
Execution always runs on a UserProxyAgent:
no matter which agent declares a tool with
, the actual execution must be registered on the agent that has
. The LLM-side agent requests the call; the proxy runs it.
Dynamic Group Chat with a Custom Selector
For teams where turn-taking cannot be predicted in advance, write a custom selector function. It receives the last speaker and the full group chat object and returns the next agent (or
to fall back to the LLM auto-picker). This gives you deterministic routing for known transitions while keeping the LLM as a fallback for novel situations.

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent, GroupChat, GroupChatManager

llm_config = {"config_list": [{"model": "gpt-4o-mini", "api_key": "sk-..."}]}

researcher = AssistantAgent("researcher", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="Research topics.", description="Use for research.")
coder = AssistantAgent("coder", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="Write code.", description="Use for coding.")
reviewer = AssistantAgent("reviewer", llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="Review code.", description="Use for review.")
user_proxy = UserProxyAgent("user_proxy", human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config=False,
    is_termination_msg=lambda m: "TERMINATE" in (m.get("content") or ""))

def smart_selector(last_speaker, groupchat):
    agents_by_name = {a.name: a for a in groupchat.agents}
    if last_speaker.name == "researcher":
        return agents_by_name["coder"]      # always code after research
    if last_speaker.name == "coder":
        return agents_by_name["reviewer"]   # always review after coding
    if last_speaker.name == "reviewer":
        return agents_by_name["user_proxy"] # hand off to proxy to run code
    return None  # let the LLM decide for any other transition

team = GroupChat(
    agents=[user_proxy, researcher, coder, reviewer],
    messages=[],
    max_round=12,
    speaker_selection_method=smart_selector,
)
manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=team, llm_config=llm_config)
user_proxy.initiate_chat(
    manager,
    message="Research quicksort and write a Python implementation.",
)
Resuming and Replaying a Team Conversation

After a group chat ends, you can inspect or replay the conversation by re-feeding the saved messages. This is useful for debugging team behaviour, comparing outputs across runs, or continuing from a checkpoint when the task was only partially completed.

import json

# Save the conversation after a run
conversation_snapshot = team.messages  # list of {role, content, name} dicts
with open("conversation_checkpoint.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(conversation_snapshot, f)

# Later: restore the conversation state
with open("conversation_checkpoint.json") as f:
    saved_messages = json.load(f)

# Replay: create a new group chat with the same agents and pre-loaded messages
replay_team = GroupChat(
    agents=[user_proxy, researcher, coder, reviewer],
    messages=saved_messages,   # seed with prior history
    max_round=6,               # only allow a few more rounds to finish the task
)
replay_manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=replay_team, llm_config=llm_config)
user_proxy.initiate_chat(
    replay_manager,
    message="Continue from the last checkpoint — the reviewer found one bug to fix.",
)

print(f"Final conversation length: {len(replay_team.messages)} messages")
Several common mistakes reduce team quality or cause infinite loops:

Overlapping system messages: if two agents have nearly identical system messages, the manager will pick one arbitrarily. Make each role's instructions mutually exclusive — the researcher never writes code; the coder never summarises facts.

No termination condition: without a TERMINATE signal in an agent's reply and a matching is_termination_msg on the proxy, the conversation runs until max_round. Always design at least one agent to write TERMINATE when the task is done.

Too large a team: every agent is included in the speaker-selection prompt. With more than 5-6 agents the prompt grows long, costs increase, and the manager may select irrelevant agents. Prefer small, focused teams with clear boundaries.

Manager without llm_config: GroupChatManager needs llm_config when speaker_selection_method='auto'. If you pass llm_config=False, it cannot select speakers — use 'round_robin' or a custom selector function instead.

Forgetting user_proxy in agents list: the UserProxyAgent must be in the GroupChat agents list to participate in the conversation and execute code. Leaving it out means code blocks are never run and the task stalls.
An agent team is a GroupChat with specialist agents and a manager — each agent has a focused system_message and a short description for routing.
Use description (separate from system_message) to guide the GroupChatManager's auto speaker selection.
Hierarchical teams nest an inner GroupChat inside an outer one via register_nested_chats — inner turns are private; only the summary surfaces.
Assign roles with non-overlapping expertise: researcher, planner, coder, reviewer, writer, executor.
A dedicated finaliser agent triggered only after all required work is done prevents premature TERMINATE signals.
allowed_or_disallowed_speaker_transitions enforces team workflow: reviewer must precede finaliser; finaliser never hands off.
Measure team efficiency post-run by counting messages per agent and comparing total rounds to max_round.
